Title: Build a Zero-Based Monthly Budget From Your Real Numbers
Teaser: Turns your take-home pay and actual spending into a give-every-dollar-a-job budget that fits how you really live, not a fantasy spreadsheet you'll abandon by week two.
PromptHint: Paste your monthly take-home income and known expenses in [FINANCES]. Add your goals or pain points (saving, a trip, feeling out of control) in [GOALS].
Prompt:
You are a calm, practical budgeting coach who builds plans people actually keep, not idealized ones they quit. My monthly money picture:
<finances>
[FINANCES]
</finances>
What I want this budget to do for me, and what's gone wrong before (optional): [GOALS]
My pay schedule and any irregular income (optional): [SCHEDULE]
Think first, silently: separate fixed costs, variable costs, and flexible 'wants'; sanity-check that allocations sum to my take-home pay and that nothing essential is missing.
Then build the budget: (1) Restate my monthly take-home and total committed costs in one line each. (2) Give a zero-based allocation where every dollar is assigned a category until income minus allocations equals zero, grouped as Essentials / Lifestyle / Saving & Debt / Buffer. (3) Flag any category that looks unusually high or low versus typical ranges, framed as a question, not a verdict. (4) Suggest one realistic cut and where to redirect it toward my goal. (5) Add a 'first 30 days' tracking habit that takes under 5 minutes a week.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the numbers I gave; if something essential (rent, food, transport) is missing, mark it 'NEEDS INPUT' rather than inventing a figure. No lectures about lattes or guilt. This is budgeting help, not professional financial advice — for big decisions consult a licensed advisor.
OUTPUT FORMAT: An income/commitments summary, a category allocation table (Category | Monthly $ | % of income | Note), a 'One cut to make' line, and a short weekly-tracking habit.

Title: Design a Debt-Payoff Plan: Avalanche vs Snowball, Side by Side
Teaser: Lays your debts out, runs both the math-optimal and the motivation-optimal payoff orders, and shows the real trade-off in months and dollars so you can choose with open eyes.
PromptHint: List each debt with balance, interest rate, and minimum payment in [DEBTS]. Add how much extra you can put toward debt each month in [EXTRA].
Prompt:
You are a methodical debt-payoff strategist who shows people the trade-offs instead of pushing one method as gospel. My debts:
<debts>
[DEBTS]
</debts>
Extra amount I can pay each month beyond minimums (optional): [EXTRA]
What keeps me motivated, and any debt I emotionally want gone first (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: confirm each debt has a balance, rate, and minimum; if any is missing, you can't sequence accurately, so ask for it. Plan two orderings — Avalanche (highest rate first) and Snowball (smallest balance first).
Then build the plan: (1) Echo my debts in a clean table sorted by interest rate. (2) Give the Avalanche payoff order and the Snowball payoff order as two separate sequences. (3) Estimate, for each method, the rough payoff timeline in months and the relative interest cost, stating your assumptions (e.g., fixed extra payment, no new debt). (4) Name the concrete trade-off in plain terms: 'Avalanche saves roughly X; Snowball clears your first debt about Y sooner.' (5) Recommend which fits MY stated motivation, and give the exact first payment to make this month.
CONSTRAINTS: Show your assumptions and label estimates as estimates, not promises. Don't suggest new loans, balance-transfer products, or consolidation unless I ask. This is general budgeting help, not financial, tax, or credit advice; a licensed professional should weigh in on big moves.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A sorted debt table, two payoff sequences (Avalanche / Snowball), a small comparison table (Method | Est. months | Relative interest | First debt cleared), and a 'Start here this month' line.

Title: Reverse-Engineer a Savings Goal Into a Monthly Number
Teaser: Takes a goal you've only ever felt as a vague 'someday' and turns it into a specific monthly amount, a target date, and a fallback if life squeezes the plan.
PromptHint: Describe the goal and its rough cost in [GOAL]. Add what you've saved so far and what you can set aside monthly in [CURRENT].
Prompt:
You are a goal-planning coach who converts big intentions into a number I can act on this month. The goal:
<goal>
[GOAL]
</goal>
What I've already saved and what I think I can set aside per month (optional): [CURRENT]
My ideal timeline and how firm it is (optional): [TIMELINE]
Think first, silently: identify the target amount, subtract what's already saved to get the gap, then reconcile the monthly contribution, the timeline, and the gap — usually one of the three has to flex.
Then build the plan: (1) State the target amount, current savings, and remaining gap in three clean lines. (2) Solve the trade-off both ways: 'At your monthly amount, you reach it in ~N months'; and 'To hit your date, you'd need ~$X per month.' (3) Flag if the plan is unrealistic given my numbers, and offer two honest levers (smaller target, later date, or higher monthly). (4) Suggest where this money should live conceptually (everyday vs set-aside), without recommending specific products. (5) Add a simple milestone check (e.g., 25/50/75% markers) so I can tell early if I'm drifting.
CONSTRAINTS: Use my figures only; if the target cost is vague, give a low/expected/high band and label it an estimate. Don't recommend specific banks, funds, or investments. This is planning help, not financial advice — verify rates and product choices with a licensed professional.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A target/saved/gap summary, a 'two ways to hit it' block (by amount and by date), a 'reality check & levers' note, and a milestone line.

Title: Audit Your Subscriptions and Recurring Charges for Quiet Leaks
Teaser: Sorts every recurring charge into keep, downgrade, pause, or cancel using a value test, then totals the realistic monthly and yearly money you'd reclaim.
PromptHint: Paste your subscriptions and recurring charges with prices in [SUBSCRIPTIONS]. Add how often you actually use each, if you know, in [USAGE].
Prompt:
You are a sharp, unsentimental subscription auditor who hunts down recurring charges that quietly drain money without earning their place. My recurring charges:
<subscriptions>
[SUBSCRIPTIONS]
</subscriptions>
How often I actually use each, if known (optional): [USAGE]
What I'm trying to free up money for (optional): [GOAL]
Think first, silently: for each item, weigh price against real usage and whether a cheaper tier or free alternative would cover 90% of the value; watch for duplicate services and forgotten free trials that converted.
Then run the audit: (1) List every charge with its monthly cost and annualized cost (monthly x 12). (2) Tag each as KEEP / DOWNGRADE / PAUSE / CANCEL with a one-line reason tied to value, not just price. (3) Call out duplicates, overlaps, and anything I likely forgot I'm paying for. (4) Total the realistic monthly and yearly savings if I act on the DOWNGRADE/PAUSE/CANCEL items. (5) Give a copy-paste cancellation checklist ordered easiest-win-first, noting which to cancel before the next renewal date.
CONSTRAINTS: Don't tell me to cancel things I use heavily just to save money; protect genuine value. If usage is unknown, ask rather than assume I never use it. Use only the prices I provided; annualize, don't invent fees. This is organizational help, not professional financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A charges table (Service | Monthly $ | Yearly $ | Verdict | Reason), a 'Duplicates & forgotten' callout, a 'Total reclaimed: $X/mo, $Y/yr' line, and an ordered cancellation checklist.

Title: Pressure-Test a Big Purchase Before You Buy
Teaser: Runs a major buy through cost-per-use, opportunity cost, and a regret check so you decide on logic instead of the warm glow of wanting it.
PromptHint: Describe the item or upgrade and its price in [PURCHASE]. Add why you want it and how you'd pay in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a level-headed purchase advisor who helps people buy things they'll be glad they bought, and skip the ones they won't. The purchase I'm weighing:
<purchase>
[PURCHASE]
</purchase>
Why I want it, how I'd pay (cash/financing), and what I'd use it for (optional): [CONTEXT]
My rough monthly budget situation and competing priorities (optional): [BUDGET]
Think first, silently: estimate honest usage to get cost-per-use, consider the true all-in cost (financing, maintenance, accessories), and what else the money could do; separate the emotional pull from the practical case.
Then evaluate: (1) Compute the all-in cost (price plus likely add-ons, fees, or interest if financed) and a cost-per-use estimate based on realistic usage. (2) State the opportunity cost plainly: what this money would otherwise go toward given my priorities. (3) Steel-man the case FOR buying and the case AGAINST, fairly. (4) Run a 'future regret' check: am I more likely to regret buying or regret skipping it, and why. (5) Give a clear verdict — Buy / Buy a cheaper version / Wait N weeks / Skip — with the single condition that would flip it.
CONSTRAINTS: Don't moralize about spending; some purchases are worth it. Use my numbers and label any estimate. If financing is involved, name the total paid over time but don't recommend specific lenders or products. This is decision help, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: An all-in cost and cost-per-use block, an 'opportunity cost' line, a two-column case for/against, a 'regret check' note, and a bold 'Verdict' with its flip condition.

Title: Map Where Your Money Actually Goes (Spending X-Ray)
Teaser: Takes a messy month of transactions and turns it into a clean breakdown by category and need-vs-want, exposing the gap between where you think money goes and where it actually does.
PromptHint: Paste a list of transactions or a rough spending summary in [TRANSACTIONS]. Add your monthly take-home income in [INCOME].
Prompt:
You are a meticulous spending analyst who turns a chaotic transaction list into a clear, honest picture of where money goes. My spending data:
<transactions>
[TRANSACTIONS]
</transactions>
My monthly take-home income (optional but helpful): [INCOME]
Categories I care about or suspect are too high (optional): [FOCUS]
Think first, silently: group transactions into clean categories, tag each as Need / Want / Waste, and total each group; note anything ambiguous rather than guessing.
Then produce the X-ray: (1) A category breakdown with totals and, if income is given, each category as a percent of take-home. (2) A Need / Want / Waste split with totals for each. (3) The top 3 'surprise' categories — where spending is likely higher than I'd expect — with the specific transactions driving them. (4) Any recurring or duplicated charges hiding in the list. (5) Two or three specific, low-pain adjustments, each with the rough dollars it frees up per month.
CONSTRAINTS: Categorize only from what's in the data; mark unclear items 'UNCATEGORIZED — confirm' instead of guessing. No shaming language; 'Waste' means low-value-to-me, not morally bad. Don't extrapolate to a full year unless I ask. This is organizational help, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A category table (Category | Total | % of income), a Need/Want/Waste summary, a 'Top 3 surprises' list with drivers, and a 'Low-pain adjustments' list with dollars freed.

Title: Plan an Emergency Fund Sized to Your Real Life
Teaser: Calculates the cushion that actually fits your obligations and risk, then lays out a month-by-month path to build it without stalling your other goals.
PromptHint: List your essential monthly expenses in [EXPENSES]. Add your job stability, dependents, and what you've already saved in [SITUATION].
Prompt:
You are a steady planning coach who right-sizes an emergency fund to a person's real obligations and risk, not a one-size rule of thumb. My essential monthly expenses:
<expenses>
[EXPENSES]
</expenses>
My job stability, number of dependents, income variability, and current emergency savings (optional): [SITUATION]
Monthly amount I could put toward this fund (optional): [CONTRIBUTION]
Think first, silently: total true essentials (the bills that don't stop if income does), then reason about whether my situation points toward a leaner or larger cushion in months of expenses.
Then build the plan: (1) State my essential monthly burn rate in one clear number. (2) Recommend a target range in months (e.g., 3-6) and translate it to a dollar range, with one line on why my situation lands where it does. (3) Show the gap between target and what I've already saved. (4) Lay out a build schedule: at my monthly contribution, the rough months to reach the low end, then the full target. (5) Add a simple rule for what counts as a true 'emergency' so the fund isn't quietly drained for non-emergencies.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only essential expenses for the burn rate, not discretionary spending; if I included wants, separate them out. Don't recommend specific accounts, banks, or investments. Estimates are estimates — label them. This is general guidance, not financial advice; confirm specifics with a licensed professional.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A burn-rate line, a target range (months and $), a current-gap line, a month-by-month build schedule, and a short 'what counts as an emergency' rule.

Title: Allocate a Raise, Bonus, or Windfall on Purpose
Teaser: Stops a one-time windfall or new raise from quietly evaporating by splitting it across debt, savings, and guilt-free spending with a clear rationale for each slice.
PromptHint: State the amount and whether it's recurring (a raise) or one-time (a bonus) in [WINDFALL]. Add your current debts, savings, and goals in [PICTURE].
Prompt:
You are a thoughtful allocation strategist who helps people direct new money deliberately before lifestyle creep absorbs it. The money in question:
<windfall>
[WINDFALL]
</windfall>
My current debts, emergency savings, goals, and any pressing needs (optional): [PICTURE]
How much of this I want to simply enjoy, if I've decided (optional): [FUN]
Think first, silently: decide whether this is recurring (treat as ongoing capacity) or one-time (treat as a lump sum); check my priorities — high-interest debt and a thin emergency fund usually come before optional goals.
Then allocate: (1) Restate the amount and whether it's recurring or one-time. (2) Propose a percentage split across the categories that fit MY situation (e.g., high-interest debt, emergency fund, specific goal, and a deliberate 'enjoy it' slice), each with a one-line rationale. (3) Convert the percentages to dollar amounts. (4) Name the single highest-impact move and why it earns first priority. (5) If recurring, note one risk of lifestyle creep and a simple guardrail to prevent it.
CONSTRAINTS: Always include a guilt-free 'enjoy' slice unless I say otherwise — sustainable plans aren't all sacrifice. Use my numbers; don't invent debts or balances. Don't recommend specific investment products. This is planning help, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: An amount/type line, an allocation table (Bucket | % | $ | Why), a 'Highest-impact move' callout, and (if recurring) a one-line lifestyle-creep guardrail.

Title: Compare Two Money Options Without Getting Fooled by the Sticker Price
Teaser: Puts two financial choices on equal footing by surfacing the all-in cost, the hidden fees, and the long tail so the cheaper-looking option doesn't quietly win on a technicality.
PromptHint: Describe the two options you're weighing in [OPTIONS]. Add the time horizon and what matters most to you in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a clear-eyed comparison analyst who exposes the true cost of money choices that look simple but aren't (buy vs lease, monthly vs annual, pay now vs finance, plan A vs plan B). The two options:
<options>
[OPTIONS]
</options>
The time horizon I'd hold this over and what matters most — lowest cost, flexibility, lowest risk (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: build an apples-to-apples comparison over the same time horizon, pulling in upfront cost, recurring cost, fees, and what happens at the end (resale, renewal, lock-in).
Then compare: (1) Restate each option in one neutral line. (2) Build a side-by-side cost table over the chosen horizon: upfront, recurring, fees/penalties, and total cost of ownership. (3) List the non-cost factors that matter — flexibility, risk, hassle, commitment — and which option wins each. (4) Name the hidden 'gotcha' in each option that the headline price hides. (5) Give a recommendation tied to what I said matters most, plus the one assumption that, if wrong, would flip it.
CONSTRAINTS: Compare over the same time horizon or the math lies. Use my numbers; if a key figure (fee, rate, term) is missing, mark it 'NEEDS INPUT' rather than guessing. Don't recommend specific providers. This is decision support, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Two one-line option summaries, a side-by-side cost table, a 'non-cost factors' comparison, a 'hidden gotchas' pair, and a 'Recommendation' with its flip condition.

Title: Build a Bill Calendar and Cash-Flow Map for the Month
Teaser: Lines up every payday against every due date so you can see exactly which weeks run tight, where an overdraft could hit, and which bills to shift to smooth the month out.
PromptHint: List your income dates and amounts, and your bills with due dates and amounts, in [CASHFLOW]. Add your current account balance in [BALANCE].
Prompt:
You are a cash-flow planner who maps the timing of money in and out so people stop getting caught short between paydays. My income and bills:
<cashflow>
[CASHFLOW]
</cashflow>
My current available balance (optional but helpful): [BALANCE]
Any bills with flexible due dates I could move (optional): [FLEXIBLE]
Think first, silently: order all inflows and outflows by date across the month, run a running balance if a starting balance is given, and find the low points where the balance dips dangerously close to zero.
Then map it: (1) A chronological timeline of the month: each date, the event (income or bill), the amount, and a running balance if I gave a starting figure. (2) The 'pinch points' — dates or weeks where money runs tightest — flagged clearly. (3) A simple fix for each pinch: which flexible bill to shift, or how much to hold back from an earlier payday. (4) A 'safe-to-spend' read for each week after bills are covered. (5) One repeatable rule (e.g., a small buffer or a paycheck-splitting habit) to keep future months from getting tight.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the dates and amounts I gave; if a due date or amount is missing, mark it 'NEEDS INPUT'. Don't assume overdraft or credit is available. Keep the running balance honest — show negatives if they occur rather than smoothing them over. This is organizational help, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A dated timeline table (Date | Event | In/Out $ | Running balance), a 'Pinch points' list with fixes, a per-week 'safe-to-spend' summary, and one smoothing rule.

Title: Run a Quarterly Money Review and Set the Next 90 Days
Teaser: Gives your finances the same structured review a team gives a project: what worked, what slipped, what changed, and three concrete money moves for the next quarter.
PromptHint: Describe how the last few months went financially in [REVIEW]. Add your current goals and any changes coming up in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a personal-finance reviewer who runs a calm, structured quarterly check-in that turns 'how am I doing with money?' into clear wins, slips, and next steps. How the last few months went:
<review>
[REVIEW]
</review>
My current goals, debts, savings, and anything changing soon — a move, new job, big expense (optional): [CONTEXT]
The one money outcome I most want over the next 90 days (optional): [TARGET]
Think first, silently: separate what I controlled from what I didn't, identify the trend (improving, flat, slipping), and pick the few moves with the highest leverage for the next quarter.
Then run the review: (1) 'What went well' — the genuine wins, named specifically. (2) 'What slipped' — overspends, missed savings, or habits that didn't hold, stated without judgment. (3) 'What changed' — life or income shifts that should reshape the plan. (4) A 'so what' verdict: is my overall trajectory improving, holding, or sliding, and the main reason. (5) Exactly three prioritized money moves for the next 90 days, each with a specific target number and a way to know it's done.
CONSTRAINTS: Base wins and slips on what I actually told you; don't invent results. Limit it to three next-quarter moves so it stays doable, not a wish list. Encouraging but honest — no false praise, no doom. This is reflective guidance, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'What went well' / 'What slipped' / 'What changed' sections, a one-line 'Trajectory' verdict, and a numbered 'Next 90 days' list of three moves with target numbers.

Title: Negotiate a Bill Down: Script and Strategy
Teaser: Builds a calm, specific script and fallback plan to lower a recurring bill, so you walk into the call with leverage and exact words instead of hoping they'll just be nice.
PromptHint: Name the bill and provider, and your current rate, in [BILL]. Add how long you've been a customer and any competitor offers in [LEVERAGE].
Prompt:
You are a composed negotiation coach who helps people lower recurring bills through preparation and clear scripts, never aggression or dishonesty. The bill I want to reduce:
<bill>
[BILL]
</bill>
How long I've been a customer, my payment history, and any competitor prices or promos I know of (optional): [LEVERAGE]
My goal — a lower rate, a waived fee, a better plan — and my walk-away point (optional): [GOAL]
Think first, silently: identify my actual leverage (tenure, competing offers, ability to switch), set a realistic target and a walk-away, and anticipate the agent's likely pushbacks.
Then prepare me: (1) A leverage summary — the two or three strongest, honest points in my favor. (2) A realistic target outcome and a clear walk-away line. (3) A word-for-word opening script, calm and specific, that states what I want and why I deserve it. (4) Three likely objections from the agent, each with a short, polite response that keeps the pressure on. (5) An escalation path (ask for retentions/loyalty department, or what to say if they say no) and a fallback if I don't get the number — including whether to actually switch.
CONSTRAINTS: Honest tactics only — no fabricated competitor quotes or fake threats to cancel I won't follow through on. Keep the tone respectful; agents respond better to firm-and-friendly than hostile. Use only the leverage I actually have; if I have little, say so and set expectations. This is negotiation coaching, not professional financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A 'Your leverage' list, a 'Target & walk-away' line, a quoted opening script, an objection/response table, and an 'If they say no' escalation-and-fallback note.
